card.intuition: Using Tarjan-style DFS, track disc[u] (discovery time) and low[u] (earliest reachable ancestor). An edge (u,v) is a bridge if low[v] > disc[u] — no back-edge bypasses it. A node u is an articulation point if it has a child v with low[v] >= disc[u] (or u is root with ≥2 children).
card.analogy: In a road network, a bridge road is one whose removal disconnects cities — no detour exists. An articulation point (city) is one whose removal splits the network into isolated regions — all traffic goes through it.
when_to_use: Network reliability: finding single points of failure
Graph analysis where removing an edge or vertex disconnects the graph
gotchas: Track parent to avoid treating the tree-edge back to parent as a back-edge
Articulation point root case: the root is an AP only if it has ≥ 2 children in the DFS tree
For multigraphs (parallel edges), track parent edge index not parent node — two parallel edges are not a bridge

exercise.prompt: Find all bridges in an undirected graph using Tarjan's algorithm.
hints: A tree edge (u,v) is a bridge when low[v] > disc[u] — the subtree rooted at v cannot reach u or any ancestor of u via a back-edge
exercise.test_code:
# Graph: 0-1-2-3, with 1-2 also reachable via back-edge 2->0? No — let's use:
# 0-1, 1-2, 2-0 (triangle), 2-3 (bridge)
adj = {0:[1,2], 1:[0,2], 2:[0,1,3], 3:[2]}
br = find_bridges(4, adj)
assert br == [(2,3)], f'Only bridge is (2,3), got {br}'

# Path graph: every edge is a bridge
adj2 = {0:[1], 1:[0,2], 2:[1,3], 3:[2]}
br2 = find_bridges(4, adj2)
assert br2 == [(0,1),(1,2),(2,3)]
print('PASS')
